enrichment.short_description: PlanGrid is a cloud and mobile app for construction blueprint management, field collaboration, punch lists and RFIs, founded in 2011 in San Francisco and acquired by Autodesk in 2018 for USD 875 million.
enrichment.company_size: Part of Autodesk, Inc. (13,000+ employees); PlanGrid itself had about 400 employees at the time of its 2018 acquisition
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, USA (now part of Autodesk, headquartered in San Rafael, California)
enrichment.seo_title: PlanGrid Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PlanGrid (now Autodesk Build) construction field management software: blueprint markup, RFIs, punch lists, pricing, features and top alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: PlanGrid is a construction field management platform built around digitized blueprints, mobile markup, RFIs (requests for information), punch lists, and daily field reports for architecture, engineering and construction (AEC) teams. It was founded in 2011 in San Francisco by Tracy Young, Ralph Gootee, Ryan Sutton-Gee, Kenny Stone and Antoine Hersen, and grew to roughly 400 employees, 12,000 customers and 120,000 paid users by the time Autodesk announced its acquisition of the company for USD 875 million in November 2018, with the deal closing in December 2018. Since the acquisition, PlanGrid's blueprint and field-collaboration technology has been folded into Autodesk Construction Cloud, and specifically into Autodesk Build (renamed Forma Build as part of Autodesk's 2026 rebrand of its construction portfolio), meaning PlanGrid is no longer sold as a fully standalone product to new customers; its capabilities now ship as part of the broader Autodesk Build subscription alongside BIM 360 field-management tools. Historically, PlanGrid used tiered per-user subscription pricing (commonly referenced as Nailgun, Dozer and Crane plans ranging from roughly USD 39 to USD 139 per user per month depending on sheet-storage limits), but current pricing for the merged Autodesk Build product is quote-based and depends on modules, project volume and user count. PlanGrid's key differentiator was always its offline-capable mobile app for viewing and marking up large-format construction drawings directly on the jobsite, with automatic version syncing once connectivity returned, plus hyperlinked sheet sets that let field crews jump between related drawings instantly. The product targets general contractors, subcontractors, architects and owners who need a single source of truth for as-built drawings, RFIs, submittals and punch-list tracking across a project's lifecycle. Its main competitors in construction field management include Procore, Fieldwire, Buildertrend, and the wider Autodesk Construction Cloud suite it was absorbed into. For SEO and directory purposes, PlanGrid remains one of the most-searched legacy brand names in construction tech, even though prospective buyers today are effectively evaluating Autodesk Build/Forma Build rather than a separately purchasable PlanGrid subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PlanGrid began in 2011 as an independent construction field management startup focused on replacing paper blueprints with a searchable, markup-friendly mobile and web app. Contractors, architects and owners used it to view large-format drawings on tablets and phones, mark up changes in the field, and keep every stakeholder working from the current version of a drawing set.

Autodesk acquired PlanGrid in December 2018 for USD 875 million, one of the largest deals in construction software history at the time. Rather than operate PlanGrid as a separate product indefinitely, Autodesk merged its field-collaboration capabilities into Autodesk Construction Cloud, and specifically into Autodesk Build (rebranded Forma Build in 2026), so the PlanGrid name today mostly describes the underlying technology and mobile app rather than a stand-alone purchasable plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Even inside Autodesk Build, the PlanGrid-derived toolset centers on digitized drawing sheets with version history, markup and hyperlinking, so field teams can pull up the latest revision of any sheet and see how it connects to related drawings, specs and RFIs without hunting through paper sets.

Field reporting tools cover punch lists, RFIs, submittals, daily logs and photo documentation, all tied back to specific sheet locations, which gives project managers and owners a traceable record of what was built, what changed, and who signed off on it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: PlanGrid is no longer sold as an independent subscription to new customers; its capabilities are packaged inside Autodesk Build (Forma Build) within Autodesk Construction Cloud, which uses custom, quote-based pricing driven by user counts, modules and project scale.

Historically, standalone PlanGrid pricing followed named tiers (often referenced as Nailgun, Dozer and Crane) priced per user per month with limits on the number of drawing sheets storable per project, with higher tiers unlocking unlimited sheets and more advanced field-management modules.

features.title: Blueprint markup and versioning
features.description: Field teams can mark up drawings on mobile or web and automatically sync changes with full version history.

features.title: Hyperlinked sheet sets
features.description: Drawings are cross-linked so users can jump directly from a callout to the referenced detail or sheet.

features.title: RFIs and submittals tracking
features.description: Requests for information and submittal packages are logged, routed and tied to specific drawing locations.

features.title: Punch lists and field reports
features.description: Teams can create, assign and close out punch-list items and daily field reports from the jobsite.

features.title: Offline mobile access
features.description: The mobile app supports offline viewing and markup, syncing automatically once a connection is restored.

features.title: Autodesk Construction Cloud integration
features.description: Data flows into the broader Autodesk Build and Autodesk Construction Cloud ecosystem, including BIM 360 tools.

pros: Purpose-built for field crews who need reliable offline access to current drawings on a jobsite
Deep integration with Autodesk's broader Construction Cloud and BIM tools after the acquisition
Long track record and strong brand recognition in the AEC industry
Hyperlinked drawing sets make navigating large sheet sets faster than paper
cons: No longer sold as a standalone product, which can confuse buyers searching for 'PlanGrid' pricing
Pricing is custom and quote-based, making cost comparison harder than tools with public pricing
Migration into Autodesk Build means some legacy PlanGrid workflows and UI have changed
Steeper learning curve and higher cost than lightweight construction apps aimed at small contractors

faqs.question: Is PlanGrid still available to buy?
faqs.answer: PlanGrid is no longer sold as an independent product to new customers. Its blueprint and field-management technology is now part of Autodesk Build (renamed Forma Build in 2026) within Autodesk Construction Cloud.

faqs.question: Who founded PlanGrid?
faqs.answer: PlanGrid was founded in 2011 in San Francisco by Tracy Young, Ralph Gootee, Ryan Sutton-Gee, Kenny Stone and Antoine Hersen.

faqs.question: When did Autodesk acquire PlanGrid?
faqs.answer: Autodesk announced the acquisition in November 2018 and completed it in December 2018 for USD 875 million.

faqs.question: How much does PlanGrid/Autodesk Build cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing is custom and quote-based through Autodesk sales; legacy standalone PlanGrid plans previously ranged from about USD 39 to USD 139 per user per month depending on the tier.

faqs.question: What is PlanGrid used for?
faqs.answer: It is used for managing construction blueprints, markups, RFIs, submittals, punch lists and daily field reports on commercial and residential construction projects.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to PlanGrid?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Procore, Fieldwire, and Buildertrend.

faqs.question: Does PlanGrid work offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, the mobile app supports offline viewing and markup of drawings, syncing changes automatically once connectivity returns.

enrichment.short_description: Planhat is a customer platform that unifies customer success, product usage and revenue data so B2B teams can track health scores, automate playbooks and manage the full customer lifecycle.
enrichment.seo_title: Planhat Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Planhat customer success and revenue platform: health scores, playbooks, pricing model, key features and top alternatives like Gainsight and ChurnZero.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Planhat is a customer platform built for customer success, product, and revenue teams that centralizes customer data, usage analytics, health scores, and automated playbooks in one system of record. The company was founded in 2014 in Stockholm, Sweden by Kaveh Rostampor and Niklas Skog, who bootstrapped the business for roughly seven years before raising a USD 50 million Series A led by Sprints Capital in 2022. Planhat has since grown into a globally distributed company with 250-plus employees across hubs including Stockholm, London, Boston and Malibu, serving customers across Europe, North America, South America and Asia-Pacific. The platform's core differentiator is its flexible, no-code data model: rather than forcing customers into a rigid schema, Planhat lets teams model their own customer objects, licenses, usage events and revenue metrics, then layer health scores, playbooks, and automated workflows on top. It has expanded beyond classic customer success monitoring into a broader 'customer platform' spanning CRM-style account management, professional services automation (PSA), and AI-assisted workflows for tasks like renewal forecasting and churn prediction. Planhat uses custom, quote-based pricing rather than published per-seat tiers; cost is generally driven by the number of managed customer accounts and which modules (CS, CRM, PSA, AI, advanced portals) a company enables, with add-ons available for advanced AI features, email marketing and expanded portal capabilities. The product targets mid-market and enterprise B2B SaaS companies that need a single source of truth for customer health, renewal risk, expansion opportunities, and cross-functional collaboration between customer success, sales, product and support teams. Typical buyers are VPs of customer success, chief customer officers, and revenue operations leaders at subscription software companies. Named competitors in the customer success and revenue platform category include Gainsight, ChurnZero, Totango and Vitally, with HubSpot's customer success tooling also competing at the margins. Because Planhat positions itself as a broader 'revenue platform' rather than a narrow CS tool, it also competes for account and relationship data with CRM platforms like Salesforce and HubSpot. Its emphasis on customizable data modeling, combined with native AI features for surfacing risk and opportunity signals, differentiates it from more rigid, template-driven customer success platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Planhat was founded in 2014 in Stockholm by Kaveh Rostampor and Niklas Skog to help subscription businesses track customer health and reduce churn. The founders bootstrapped the company for about seven years before raising outside funding, building a product used by customer success, product and revenue teams at B2B software companies.

Today Planhat markets itself as a 'customer platform' spanning customer success management (CSM), a lightweight CRM, professional services automation and AI-assisted workflows, aiming to be a single system of record for how a company manages its existing customer base.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Planhat's data model is flexible and no-code: teams can define their own objects (accounts, licenses, products, usage events) rather than being locked into a fixed schema, then build custom health scores and dashboards on top of that data.

Automation features include playbooks that trigger tasks, alerts or outreach based on usage drops, renewal dates or health-score changes, along with AI features aimed at surfacing churn risk, expansion opportunities and renewal forecasts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Planhat does not publish fixed per-seat pricing. Instead it uses a quote-based model that is primarily driven by the number of active customer accounts a company manages and which modules (CS, CRM, PSA, AI, advanced portals) are enabled, rather than a strict per-user seat count.

Add-ons such as an upgraded AI platform, advanced service and PSA tools, email marketing, and advanced customer portals are priced separately, and enterprise buyers typically work with Planhat's sales team on implementation and onboarding as part of the contract.

features.title: Customer health scoring
features.description: Configurable health scores combine usage, support and engagement data to flag at-risk or high-opportunity accounts.

features.title: No-code data modeling
features.description: Teams can define custom objects and fields to match their own customer, product and revenue structure.

features.title: Automated playbooks
features.description: Trigger-based workflows automate tasks, alerts and outreach tied to account milestones or risk signals.

features.title: Revenue and renewal tracking
features.description: Tracks ARR, renewals, upsell and expansion opportunities alongside customer health data.

features.title: Customer portals
features.description: Branded portals let customers collaborate on shared plans, tasks and milestones with their vendor.

features.title: AI-assisted insights
features.description: AI features help surface churn risk, renewal forecasts and account opportunities from usage and engagement data.

features.title: PSA and services module
features.description: Professional services automation tools support project and services delivery tracking for customer-facing teams.

pros: Highly flexible, no-code data model adapts to different business structures without custom development
Combines customer success, light CRM and PSA in one platform, reducing tool sprawl
Strong automation and playbook capabilities for scaling customer success operations
Backed by a bootstrapped, profitable history before raising funding, suggesting strong product-market fit
cons: No public pricing, making it hard to budget or compare without a sales conversation
Flexible data model can require significant setup and configuration time to get right
Feature breadth (CS plus CRM plus PSA plus AI) may be more than smaller teams need
Enterprise-oriented pricing can be costly for very small customer success teams

faqs.question: What is Planhat used for?
faqs.answer: Planhat is used by customer success, product and revenue teams to track customer health, automate renewal and onboarding workflows, and manage the overall customer lifecycle.

faqs.question: Who founded Planhat and when?
faqs.answer: Planhat was founded in 2014 in Stockholm, Sweden by Kaveh Rostampor and Niklas Skog.

faqs.question: How much does Planhat cost?
faqs.answer: Planhat uses custom, quote-based pricing driven by the number of managed customer accounts and enabled modules; there is no published per-seat price list.

faqs.question: Is Planhat a CRM?
faqs.answer: Planhat includes lightweight CRM-style account management features, but it is primarily built as a customer success and revenue platform rather than a full sales CRM replacement.

faqs.question: What companies compete with Planhat?
faqs.answer: Common competitors include Gainsight, ChurnZero, Totango and Vitally.

faqs.question: Does Planhat use AI?
faqs.answer: Yes, Planhat offers AI-assisted features for surfacing churn risk, renewal forecasts and account opportunities, with an upgraded AI platform available as an add-on.

faqs.question: Is Planhat suitable for small businesses?
faqs.answer: Planhat is generally positioned toward mid-market and enterprise B2B companies with dedicated customer success teams rather than very small businesses.

enrichment.short_description: Planka is a self-hosted, real-time Kanban board built as an open, fair-code alternative to Trello, developed by PLANKA Software GmbH in Germany.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted Community Edition); Pro plans priced separately
enrichment.company_size: Small team (PLANKA Software GmbH; exact team size not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_title: Planka Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Planka self-hosted Kanban board: real-time boards, Docker deployment, Community vs Pro pricing, features and alternatives like Trello and Wekan compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Planka is a self-hosted, real-time Kanban board application positioned as an open alternative to Trello, developed by PLANKA Software GmbH, a company headquartered in Kempten, Germany. The open-source Community Edition, hosted on GitHub since 2019 and accumulating more than 11,000 stars, is free to self-host via Docker or other deployment methods and gives teams full control over their data and infrastructure. Planka's code is source-available under a 'Fair Use License' (a fair-code license rather than a traditional OSI-approved open-source license), which permits free use for personal, internal-organizational, non-profit and educational purposes but restricts operating Planka as a hosted service for third parties or sharing a single license across unrelated companies without a commercial Pro license. In 2025 the company introduced a Pro Edition aimed at teams that need higher scalability, advanced professional features and premium support, available either self-hosted or as a fully managed cloud offering, alongside a paid installation service (starting around EUR 469 plus VAT) for teams that want PLANKA configured on their own server or cloud environment. Core functionality includes unlimited boards and cards, real-time synchronization across users, background and card customization, and an interface that has been translated into 35-plus languages by its community. Planka targets small to mid-sized teams, privacy-conscious organizations, and self-hosting enthusiasts who want Trello-style Kanban boards without sending data to a third-party SaaS vendor, as well as agencies and companies that prefer to run project-management tools inside their own infrastructure for compliance or cost reasons. Its closest competitors in the self-hosted Kanban space include Wekan (a fully open-source, MIT-licensed alternative) and Focalboard, and, in the broader hosted Kanban category, Trello itself along with Jira and Asana's board views. Planka differentiates itself with a clean, modern interface closely modeled on Trello's UX, straightforward Docker-based deployment, and a licensing model that keeps the core product free for internal use while monetizing hosting, scale and support through its newer Pro tier rather than gating basic Kanban functionality behind a paywall.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Planka is a Kanban-style project and task management tool designed to be self-hosted, giving teams a Trello-like board experience while keeping full ownership of their data. The project has been developed publicly on GitHub since 2019 by PLANKA Software GmbH, based in Kempten, Germany, and has grown to more than 11,000 GitHub stars.

The Community Edition is source-available and free to run on your own infrastructure, typically via Docker. In 2025 the company launched a Pro Edition for teams that need greater scalability, additional professional features, and premium support, offered either self-hosted or as a managed cloud service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Planka supports unlimited boards, lists and cards with real-time updates, so changes made by one team member appear instantly for everyone else viewing the same board, similar to the live-collaboration feel of hosted tools like Trello.

Because it is self-hosted, teams get full control over where their data lives, which matters for organizations with data residency, privacy or compliance requirements. The interface has also been translated into more than 35 languages through community contributions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community Edition is completely free to self-host under Planka's fair-code 'Fair Use License,' which allows personal, internal, non-profit and educational use but restricts reselling Planka as a hosted service to third parties without a commercial license.

The newer Pro Edition adds scalability and support features and is available self-hosted or fully managed; PLANKA also offers a paid installation and configuration service, starting at roughly EUR 469 plus VAT, for teams that want assistance deploying it on their own server or cloud environment.

features.title: Real-time Kanban boards
features.description: Boards, lists and cards update live for all connected users without needing to refresh the page.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs on your own server or cloud environment via Docker, keeping full control over data.

features.title: Unlimited boards and cards
features.description: The Community Edition places no artificial limits on the number of boards or cards a team can create.

features.title: Multi-language interface
features.description: The interface has been translated into 35-plus languages by the community.

features.title: Pro Edition scalability
features.description: A paid Pro tier adds higher scalability, advanced features and premium support for larger teams.

features.title: Managed cloud or self-hosted Pro
features.description: Pro Edition customers can choose between self-managing infrastructure or a fully managed cloud deployment.

pros: Free to self-host with no per-user fees for the Community Edition
Familiar, Trello-like interface with a low learning curve
Real-time collaboration and unlimited boards and cards
Full data ownership, appealing to privacy- and compliance-conscious teams
Active open development community with frequent updates and many translations
cons: Self-hosting requires basic server or Docker administration skills
The Fair Use License is not an OSI-approved open-source license and restricts reselling Planka as a hosted service
Pro Edition pricing is not fully transparent on the public website
Lacks some advanced automation and reporting features found in larger commercial PM tools

faqs.question: Is Planka really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community Edition is free to self-host, though a paid Pro Edition with added scalability and support is also available.

faqs.question: Is Planka open source?
faqs.answer: Planka's source code is publicly available on GitHub, but it is distributed under a fair-code 'Fair Use License' rather than a traditional OSI-approved open-source license.

faqs.question: How do I install Planka?
faqs.answer: Planka is typically deployed via Docker on your own server or cloud provider; PLANKA also offers a paid installation service if you want it set up for you.

faqs.question: Who makes Planka?
faqs.answer: Planka is developed by PLANKA Software GmbH, based in Kempten, Germany.

faqs.question: What is Planka's main alternative?
faqs.answer: Trello is the closest hosted alternative, while Wekan is a fully open-source, self-hosted alternative in the same category.

faqs.question: Can I use Planka to host boards for other companies as a service?
faqs.answer: No, the Fair Use License prohibits operating Planka as a hosted service for third parties without a commercial Pro license.

faqs.question: Does Planka support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, its interface has been translated into more than 35 languages through community contributions.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious project tracking
use_cases.description: Teams that need to keep task data on their own infrastructure self-host Planka instead of using a third-party SaaS Kanban tool.

use_cases.title: Small team Kanban workflow
use_cases.description: Startups and small teams use Planka as a free Trello alternative to organize sprints, backlogs and to-do lists.

use_cases.title: Internal agency task boards
use_cases.description: Agencies run Planka internally to track client work and deliverables without paying per-seat SaaS fees.

enrichment.short_description: Plann is a visual social media scheduling and content planning tool founded in Sydney in 2016, acquired by Linktree in 2024, supporting Instagram, TikTok, Facebook, LinkedIn, Pinterest and YouTube Shorts.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (limited plan); paid plans from $12.50/month billed annually
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (part of Linktree, which has approximately 290 employees)
enrichment.headquarters: Sydney, Australia (operating as part of Linktree, headquartered in Collingwood, Australia)
enrichment.seo_title: Plann Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Plann social media scheduler: visual content planning, AI captions, pricing plans, features and alternatives like Later and Buffer compared for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Plann is a visual social media scheduling and content-planning tool founded in 2016 in Sydney, Australia by former advertising executive Christy Laurence. The platform lets creators, small businesses and marketing teams visually plan, schedule and analyze content across Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, LinkedIn, Pinterest, YouTube Shorts and Threads from a single calendar-style dashboard, with an emphasis on Instagram grid planning, AI-assisted captions and hashtag suggestions, and best-time-to-post recommendations. Plann grew to serve more than three million brands across over 160 countries before being acquired by Linktree, the Australian link-in-bio company, in August 2024 for an undisclosed sum; the entire Plann team, including founder Christy Laurence, joined Linktree as part of the deal, positioning Plann as Linktree's social scheduling and content-planning layer alongside its link-in-bio product. Plann uses a freemium pricing model: a free plan covers a single brand and user with limited monthly posts, stories, carousels and a capped number of AI credits, while paid tiers unlock unlimited scheduling across all supported platforms. As of 2026, paid plans include Power User at roughly USD 12.50 per month billed annually (USD 15 monthly) for one brand and one user with AI tools, strategy builder and hashtag suggestions; Build at roughly USD 25 per month annually (USD 30 monthly) for two brands and two users with team chat, approval flows and shareable client links; and Grow at roughly USD 62.50 per month annually (USD 75 monthly) for five brands and four users with advanced analytics and expanded team collaboration. The product is aimed at solo creators, small business owners, social media managers and marketing agencies that need to plan content visually (especially Instagram grid layout) rather than just queue posts in a list. Plann's main competitors in the social media scheduling category include Later, Buffer, Hootsuite, and Sprout Social, with its visual grid-planning focus and Instagram-first design historically differentiating it from more feed-agnostic competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Plann was founded in 2016 in Sydney by Christy Laurence to help brands and creators visually plan their Instagram grids before posting, rather than scheduling content blindly. The tool grew into a broader multi-platform scheduler used by more than three million brands worldwide.

In August 2024, Plann was acquired by Linktree, the Melbourne-based link-in-bio company, with the entire Plann team joining Linktree. The acquisition combined Linktree's link-in-bio distribution with Plann's content planning and scheduling tools under one company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Plann's core feature is visual content planning: users drag and drop posts onto a grid or calendar to preview how an Instagram feed will look before anything goes live, alongside AI-generated captions and hashtag suggestions to speed up content creation.

Beyond Instagram, Plann schedules content to Facebook, TikTok, LinkedIn, Pinterest, YouTube Shorts and Threads, and includes team collaboration features like approval workflows, shareable client links and analytics on paid plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Plann offers a free plan limited to one brand, one user, a capped number of AI credits, and Instagram-only scheduling with a maximum of five video posts per month.

Paid plans scale from Power User (one brand, one user) through Build (two brands, two users, team features) to Grow (five brands, four users, advanced analytics), priced from roughly USD 12.50 to USD 62.50 per month when billed annually, with all paid tiers covering the full range of supported platforms.

features.title: Analytics and strategy builder
features.description: Reporting tools and a strategy builder help track performance and plan content themes.

features.title: Multi-brand management
features.description: Higher plans support managing multiple brands or client accounts from one account.

Supports a wide range of platforms including TikTok, LinkedIn, Pinterest and YouTube Shorts
Usable free plan for testing before committing to a paid tier
Team and agency features like approval flows and client links on higher plans
Backed by Linktree's resources following the 2024 acquisition
cons: Free plan is limited to Instagram scheduling only, capping its usefulness for multi-platform creators
Higher tiers needed to unlock multi-brand and team collaboration features
AI credits are capped on lower tiers, limiting how much AI content generation is available
Smaller ecosystem and integration library compared to larger competitors like Hootsuite

faqs.question: What is Plann used for?

faqs.question: Who owns Plann now?
faqs.answer: Plann was acquired by Linktree in August 2024 and now operates as part of Linktree's product suite.

faqs.question: How much does Plann cost?

faqs.question: Who founded Plann?
faqs.answer: Plann was founded in 2016 in Sydney, Australia by Christy Laurence.

enrichment.short_description: PlantUML is a free, open-source tool that generates UML and other technical diagrams from plain-text descriptions, first released in 2009 by developer Arnaud Roques.
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable (open-source project maintained by a small core team led by Arnaud Roques, with community contributors)
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable (open-source project; no formal company headquarters)
enrichment.seo_title: PlantUML Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PlantUML open-source diagram tool: text-to-UML syntax, sequence and class diagrams, free GPL license, features and alternatives like Mermaid compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: PlantUML is a free, open-source diagramming tool that lets developers and technical writers generate UML and other technical diagrams from plain-text descriptions instead of dragging shapes in a graphical editor. It was created by French developer Arnaud Roques and first released in 2009, and has since grown into one of the most widely used text-to-diagram tools in software engineering, distributed under the GNU General Public License (GPL) as free software that users can redistribute and modify. PlantUML is not a commercial company but a community-driven open-source project hosted on GitHub, maintained primarily by Roques along with a network of volunteer contributors; there is no headquarters, funding round or employee count to report because it operates as free software rather than a SaaS business. The tool's core differentiator is its plain-text syntax: a short block of PlantUML markup can describe a sequence diagram, class diagram, use case diagram, activity diagram, component diagram, state diagram, or deployment diagram, which PlantUML then renders as an image (PNG, SVG or LaTeX), using the underlying Graphviz layout engine for many diagram types. Beyond classic UML, PlantUML has expanded to support additional diagram formats including ArchiMate, BPMN-style flows, C4 model diagrams, Gantt charts, mind maps, wireframes, entity-relationship diagrams, network diagrams, and visualization of raw JSON and YAML data. Because diagrams are just text, they are easy to store in version control, diff between commits, generate programmatically, and embed directly in Markdown files, wikis, and documentation-as-code pipelines, which has made PlantUML popular for architecture decision records, API documentation and onboarding docs. It can be run as a local Java application, a server (PlantUML Server), or invoked from countless editor and CI integrations, including plugins for VS Code, IntelliJ, Confluence, and static site generators. Its target users are software engineers, architects, and technical writers who prefer diagrams-as-code workflows over manual drawing tools. Its main competitors in the text-to-diagram category include Mermaid (which is more tightly integrated into GitHub and Markdown renderers by default), Graphviz/DOT for lower-level graph rendering, Draw.io (diagrams.net) for manual drag-and-drop diagramming, and Lucidchart or Structurizr for more visual, collaborative diagramming workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PlantUML was first released in 2009 by developer Arnaud Roques as a way to generate UML diagrams directly from plain-text descriptions rather than manual drag-and-drop editors. It has since become one of the most widely adopted diagrams-as-code tools in software development.

As an open-source project distributed under the GPL license, PlantUML has no company, funding, or commercial pricing behind it. It is maintained by its original author alongside a community of contributors on GitHub, and is free to download, self-host, or use through public and private PlantUML servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: PlantUML supports the full range of classic UML diagram types, including sequence, class, use case, activity, component, state and deployment diagrams, all defined with a concise, human-readable text syntax.

Beyond UML, it supports ArchiMate, BPMN-style diagrams, C4 model diagrams, Gantt charts, mind maps, wireframes, entity-relationship diagrams and network diagrams, plus direct visualization of JSON and YAML data, making it useful well beyond strict UML modeling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: PlantUML is entirely free and open source under the GPL license. There are no paid tiers, subscriptions, or feature gates for the core tool itself.

Costs, if any, come from optional infrastructure choices, such as running a self-hosted PlantUML server at scale or paying for third-party plugins, hosting, or documentation platforms that embed PlantUML rendering, rather than from PlantUML itself.

features.title: Text-to-diagram syntax
features.description: Diagrams are written as plain text and rendered automatically, making them easy to version and diff.

features.title: Broad UML diagram support
features.description: Covers sequence, class, use case, activity, component, state and deployment diagrams.

features.title: Extended diagram types
features.description: Also supports ArchiMate, BPMN-style flows, C4 model diagrams, Gantt charts and mind maps.

features.title: JSON and YAML visualization
features.description: Can render structured JSON and YAML data directly as visual diagrams.

features.title: Editor and CI integrations
features.description: Plugins exist for VS Code, IntelliJ, Confluence and many static site generators and CI pipelines.

features.title: Self-hostable server
features.description: Can be run locally, as a self-hosted PlantUML Server, or accessed via public rendering servers.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers
Diagrams-as-code approach fits naturally into version control and documentation-as-code workflows
Extremely broad diagram type support beyond just UML
Large ecosystem of editor, wiki and CI integrations
Long-running, mature project with an active community
cons: Text-based syntax has a learning curve compared to drag-and-drop diagram tools
Fine-grained visual styling and layout control is more limited than dedicated design tools
Relies on Java and, for some diagram types, Graphviz, which adds setup steps for self-hosting
No official commercial support plans, so enterprise users rely on community help or third-party vendors

faqs.question: Is PlantUML free?
faqs.answer: Yes, PlantUML is completely free and open source, distributed under the GPL license.

faqs.question: Who created PlantUML?
faqs.answer: PlantUML was created by developer Arnaud Roques and first released in 2009.

faqs.question: What diagram types does PlantUML support?
faqs.answer: It supports UML diagrams like sequence, class, use case, activity and state diagrams, plus ArchiMate, BPMN-style flows, C4 diagrams, Gantt charts, mind maps and more.

faqs.question: Can I self-host PlantUML?
faqs.answer: Yes, PlantUML can be run locally, as a self-hosted PlantUML Server, or accessed through public rendering servers.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PlantUML and Mermaid?
faqs.answer: Both are text-to-diagram tools, but Mermaid renders natively inside GitHub and many Markdown viewers by default, while PlantUML supports a broader range of diagram types and typically requires a rendering server or plugin.

faqs.question: Does PlantUML require Graphviz?
faqs.answer: Some PlantUML diagram types rely on the Graphviz layout engine for rendering, so self-hosted setups often need Graphviz installed.

faqs.question: Can PlantUML diagrams be embedded in documentation?
faqs.answer: Yes, PlantUML integrates with tools like Confluence, VS Code, IntelliJ and many documentation and static site generators.

use_cases.title: Software architecture documentation
use_cases.description: Engineering teams document system architecture and sequence flows as version-controlled text alongside their code.

use_cases.title: API and onboarding documentation
use_cases.description: Technical writers embed generated diagrams directly into Markdown docs and wikis to explain system behavior.
use_cases.audience: Technical writers and developer relations teams

use_cases.title: Automated diagram generation in CI
use_cases.description: Teams generate up-to-date architecture or dependency diagrams automatically as part of build pipelines.

features: Full UML and extended diagram support

enrichment.short_description: Planview is an enterprise portfolio and work management software company founded in 1989 in Austin, Texas, offering strategic planning, project portfolio management (PPM) and value stream management tools.
enrichment.seo_title: Planview Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Planview enterprise PPM and work management software: portfolio planning, resource management, pricing model, features and alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Planview is an enterprise software company specializing in project and portfolio management (PPM), strategic planning, and value stream management for large organizations. It was founded in 1989 in Austin, Texas by Patrick Durbin, who led the company through its first 15 years before Greg Gilmore became CEO in 2004; more recent leadership includes Razat Gaurav, who served as CEO before Matt Zilli was named CEO in January 2026. Planview's flagship product line grew out of Planview Enterprise, a web-based resource and portfolio management platform launched in 1997, and the company has since expanded aggressively through acquisitions, including Troux Technologies (2015), Innotas (2016), LeanKit (2017), Spigit (2018), Aptage (2020), Clarizen and Changepoint (2021, forming Planview AdaptiveWork), Tasktop (2022), Enrich (2022), and Plutora (2024), building a broad portfolio spanning strategic portfolio management, agile program management, product portfolio management, and value stream management. Ownership has also changed hands over the years: private equity firm Thoma Bravo held a majority stake before TPG, TA Associates and HarbourVest Partners acquired Planview from Thoma Bravo in a deal valued at USD 1.6 billion, completed in 2020, with Thoma Bravo retaining a minority interest. Planview does not publish standard pricing; it sells custom, quote-based enterprise contracts priced according to user counts, modules and deployment scale, typically negotiated directly with its sales team, which puts it out of reach for very small teams and squarely targets large enterprises, PMOs (project management offices), and IT and product organizations managing complex, multi-team portfolios. Its differentiators include breadth across the entire portfolio-to-delivery lifecycle (from top-down strategic planning down to team-level agile execution and value stream metrics) and its Tasktop-derived value stream management and integration capabilities, which connect disparate tools like Jira, Azure DevOps and ServiceNow into a unified delivery pipeline view. Named competitors include Broadcom's Clarity PPM, Microsoft Project and Project for the web, monday.com, Smartsheet, and Jira Align (Atlassian), with Planview generally competing at the higher end of enterprise PPM and portfolio management deals against Broadcom Clarity in particular.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Planview was founded in 1989 in Austin, Texas by Patrick Durbin and grew into one of the longest-running vendors in the project and portfolio management (PPM) space, launching its flagship web-based Planview Enterprise platform in 1997.

Over the past decade Planview expanded significantly through acquisitions, including LeanKit, Clarizen, Changepoint, Tasktop and Plutora, broadening its portfolio from classic top-down PPM into agile program management, value stream management and toolchain integration for software delivery.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Planview's product suite spans strategic portfolio management, resource and capacity planning, project and program management, agile program management, and product portfolio management, letting large organizations plan work at multiple altitudes from executive strategy down to team-level delivery.

Its value stream management capabilities, strengthened by the 2022 Tasktop acquisition, connect tools like Jira, Azure DevOps, ServiceNow and GitHub into a single pipeline view, giving leadership visibility into software delivery flow, bottlenecks and cycle time across many disconnected teams and tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Planview does not publish standard pricing for its products. Costs are negotiated directly with its sales team and are based on the specific modules selected, number of users, and deployment scale, typical of enterprise PPM software.
Because Planview targets large enterprises and PMOs rather than small teams, contracts are generally structured as multi-year enterprise agreements rather than simple monthly per-seat subscriptions.

features.title: Strategic portfolio management
features.description: Aligns top-down strategic objectives with the projects, programs and investments funded to deliver them.

features.title: Resource and capacity planning
features.description: Tracks resource allocation and capacity across projects and programs to avoid overcommitment.

features.title: Agile program management
features.description: Supports scaled agile frameworks and cross-team program coordination alongside traditional PPM.

features.title: Value stream management
features.description: Connects delivery tools like Jira and Azure DevOps to visualize flow and bottlenecks across the software delivery pipeline.

features.title: Product portfolio management
features.description: Helps product organizations prioritize and track investment across a portfolio of products and features.

features.title: Reporting and dashboards
features.description: Provides executive-level dashboards and reporting across portfolios, programs and resource utilization.

pros: Extremely broad functional coverage spanning strategy, portfolio, program and delivery-level management
Long track record and deep enterprise PPM experience dating back to 1989
Strong value stream management and toolchain integration capabilities via the Tasktop acquisition
Suited to complex, multi-team, multi-tool enterprise environments
cons: No public pricing, requiring a sales conversation for even a rough cost estimate
Complexity and breadth can be overkill for small or mid-sized teams
Product line assembled through many acquisitions can feel fragmented across modules
Steeper implementation and onboarding effort compared to lightweight PM tools

faqs.question: What does Planview do?
faqs.answer: Planview provides enterprise software for strategic portfolio management, project and program management, resource planning, and value stream management.

faqs.question: When was Planview founded?
faqs.answer: Planview was founded in 1989 in Austin, Texas by Patrick Durbin.

faqs.question: How much does Planview cost?
faqs.answer: Planview uses custom, quote-based enterprise pricing negotiated with its sales team based on modules, users and deployment scale; it does not publish fixed prices.

faqs.question: Who owns Planview?
faqs.answer: Planview is owned by private equity firms TPG and TA Associates, following a 2020 acquisition from Thoma Bravo valued at USD 1.6 billion, with Thoma Bravo retaining a minority stake.

faqs.question: What is Planview AdaptiveWork?
faqs.answer: Planview AdaptiveWork is the product line formed from Planview's 2021 acquisitions of Clarizen and Changepoint, focused on work and project management.

faqs.question: Who are Planview's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Key competitors include Broadcom Clarity PPM, monday.com, Smartsheet, and Jira Align.

faqs.question: Is Planview suitable for small businesses?
faqs.answer: Planview is primarily built for large enterprises and PMOs managing complex, multi-team portfolios rather than small businesses.

use_cases.description: PMOs use Planview to align strategic objectives with funded projects and track portfolio-level investment.

use_cases.description: IT leaders connect Jira, Azure DevOps and other tools through Planview to see flow and bottlenecks across delivery pipelines.

use_cases.title: Resource capacity planning across programs
use_cases.description: Program managers track resource allocation across multiple concurrent projects to prevent overcommitment.

features: Value stream management and toolchain integrations

enrichment.short_description: Plasmic is a visual, no-code web builder for developers that generates production-ready code and integrates directly into existing React codebases, founded in San Francisco in 2019.
enrichment.seo_title: Plasmic Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Plasmic visual builder for developers: drag-and-drop UI design, React code generation, pricing plans, features and alternatives like Webflow compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Plasmic is a visual, no-code website and app builder aimed at developers, letting designers and marketers drag-and-drop UI directly inside an existing React codebase while generating clean, production-ready code rather than locking content into a proprietary hosted platform. It was founded in 2019 in San Francisco by CEO Yang Zhang, who holds a PhD from MIT's Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory and previously worked at Google Research, Microsoft Research and Yahoo Labs, alongside co-founder and CTO Chung Wu. The company is backed by a seed funding round from investors including NEA, Tiny Capital, Designer Fund, Prime Set, #Angels and Arrive, and operates as a small team of roughly 10-12 employees. Plasmic's core differentiator is that it is 'headless': rather than forcing teams onto a fully hosted, closed platform the way many no-code website builders do, it embeds a visual design canvas directly into a developer's own codebase (React, Next.js and other frameworks), so marketers and designers can edit pages and components visually while engineers keep full control over the underlying code, data fetching, and business logic. It also ships as a standalone visual CMS and page builder for teams that want a fully hosted option without wiring it into custom code. Plasmic uses freemium pricing: a Free tier supports unlimited projects and a headless CMS with up to three collaborators; Starter (around USD 39 per month billed yearly) removes the Plasmic badge and adds a custom favicon and AI support chat; Pro (around USD 103 per month billed yearly, its most popular tier) adds 4-10 collaborators and unlimited AI access; Scale (around USD 399 per month billed yearly) adds content creator mode, A/B testing and scheduled content for larger teams; and Enterprise offers custom pricing with SSO, whitelabeling, embedding and dedicated support. Plasmic targets product teams, marketing teams working alongside engineers, and agencies that want marketers to self-serve on landing pages and content without waiting on developer cycles, while keeping the codebase clean and framework-native. Its main competitors include Webflow (a more fully hosted, closed visual builder), Builder.io (a similarly headless, code-embeddable visual editor), Framer, and low-code app builders like Retool for internal tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Plasmic was founded in 2019 in San Francisco by CEO Yang Zhang, a former Google Research and Microsoft Research engineer with a PhD from MIT, and CTO Chung Wu. The company set out to solve a common tension between marketing and engineering teams: marketers want to edit pages visually, while developers want clean, maintainable code.

Plasmic's answer is a headless visual builder that plugs directly into an existing codebase, most commonly React or Next.js projects, so non-engineers can build and edit UI visually while the underlying code stays framework-native and under version control rather than trapped inside a proprietary platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The visual canvas supports drag-and-drop page and component design, with the ability to bind design elements to real application data and logic defined by developers, so visual edits stay connected to live functionality rather than becoming static mockups.

Plasmic also ships a built-in headless CMS, AI-assisted design and content tools, and support for A/B testing and scheduled content publishing on higher tiers, making it usable both as a marketing site builder and as an internal visual editing layer for product teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Plasmic's Free tier includes unlimited projects and a headless CMS for up to three collaborators, making it accessible for individuals and small projects to get started at no cost.

Paid tiers scale from Starter (around USD 39 per month billed yearly) through Pro (around USD 103 per month) to Scale (around USD 399 per month), adding more collaborators, AI access, A/B testing and content scheduling, with custom Enterprise pricing for SSO, whitelabeling and dedicated support.

features.title: Visual drag-and-drop canvas
features.description: Designers and marketers build and edit UI visually without writing code.

features.title: Codebase integration
features.description: Generates production-ready code that plugs directly into existing React and Next.js projects.

features.description: Includes a built-in content management system that can be used with or without the visual builder.

features.title: AI-assisted design
features.description: AI tools help generate and edit content and components within the visual builder.

features.title: A/B testing and scheduled content
features.description: Higher-tier plans support content experimentation and scheduled publishing.

features.title: Component and data binding
features.description: Visual elements can bind to real application data and logic defined by developers.

pros: Generates clean, framework-native code instead of locking content into a proprietary platform
Lets marketers and designers edit pages visually without waiting on engineering
Usable free tier with unlimited projects for getting started
Flexible enough to work as a headless CMS alone or a full visual builder embedded in code
cons: Smaller company and team size than larger no-code competitors like Webflow
Requires some developer setup to integrate deeply into an existing codebase
Additional collaborators cost extra on Pro and Scale tiers
Learning curve for teams unfamiliar with headless, code-integrated visual builders

faqs.question: What is Plasmic used for?
faqs.answer: Plasmic is used to visually build and edit web pages and UI components that generate production-ready code, typically integrated into an existing React or Next.js codebase.

faqs.question: Who founded Plasmic?
faqs.answer: Plasmic was founded in 2019 in San Francisco by CEO Yang Zhang and CTO Chung Wu.

faqs.question: Is Plasmic free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Plasmic offers a free tier with unlimited projects and a headless CMS for up to three collaborators; paid plans start at around USD 39 per month billed yearly.

faqs.question: Does Plasmic work with React?
faqs.answer: Yes, Plasmic is designed to integrate directly into React and Next.js codebases, generating code that developers can review and extend.

faqs.question: Is Plasmic a no-code tool?
faqs.answer: Plasmic is a visual, low-code/no-code builder aimed at letting non-developers edit UI, while still producing real code that developers control.

faqs.question: What are Plasmic's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Key competitors include Webflow, Builder.io and Framer.

faqs.question: Does Plasmic have a CMS?
faqs.answer: Yes, Plasmic includes a built-in headless CMS that can be used independently of the visual page builder.

enrichment.short_description: Plausible Analytics is an open-source, privacy-friendly, cookieless web analytics platform founded in Estonia in 2018, offering a lightweight alternative to Google Analytics without tracking personal data.
enrichment.seo_title: Plausible Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Plausible Analytics: open-source, cookieless web analytics with no personal data tracking. Pricing plans, features and alternatives to Google Analytics.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Plausible Analytics is an open-source, privacy-focused web analytics platform built as a lightweight, cookieless alternative to Google Analytics. It was founded by Uku Taht, who started the project in December 2018 and launched paid subscriptions in 2019, with Marko Saric joining in 2020 to lead marketing and communications; the company, legally registered as Plausible Insights OU, is headquartered in Tartu, Estonia, and operates as a small, self-funded, profitable team of around 10 people with no outside venture investors. Plausible's core differentiator is privacy: it does not use cookies, does not collect or store any personally identifiable information, and does not track users across websites, which lets sites using it avoid cookie consent banners in many jurisdictions while still complying with GDPR and CCPA; all data is hosted and processed within the EU. The product is fully open source, licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3 (AGPLv3), and can be either self-hosted for free or run as a managed cloud SaaS. Plausible's cloud pricing is based on monthly pageviews rather than per-seat licensing: the Starter plan begins at USD 9 per month for up to 10,000 monthly pageviews with a single site and three years of data retention, the Growth plan begins at USD 14 per month and adds multiple sites and team members, and the Business plan begins at USD 19 per month and adds custom properties, the Stats API, funnels, revenue attribution and a Looker Studio connector, with all tiers scaling in price as pageview volume grows and custom Enterprise pricing available for sites with 10 million-plus monthly visitors. The platform targets developers, indie hackers, privacy-conscious businesses, and organizations in regulated industries that want simple, GDPR-friendly traffic analytics without the complexity, cookie banners, or data-privacy concerns of Google Analytics. Reported customers include Basecamp, Ghost, Hugging Face, MongoDB, the Python Software Foundation and Harvard University. Its main competitors in the privacy-focused analytics category include Fathom Analytics, Simple Analytics, Matomo, and Umami, alongside Google Analytics as the dominant incumbent it positions itself against.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Plausible Analytics was started by Uku Taht in December 2018 as a response to what its founders saw as web analytics tools that had drifted from their original purpose of simply understanding site traffic into invasive, ad-driven surveillance of visitors. Paid subscriptions launched in 2019, and Marko Saric joined in 2020 to help lead growth and communications.

The company, Plausible Insights OU, is based in Tartu, Estonia, operates as a small, self-funded, profitable team of about 10 people, and has deliberately avoided outside venture funding, relying entirely on subscription revenue from its hosted product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Plausible tracks essential website metrics such as visitors, page views, referral sources, top pages, bounce rate and device type, all shown on a single, simple dashboard, without using cookies, fingerprinting, or any form of cross-site tracking.

Because it collects no personal data, sites using Plausible generally do not need to show a cookie consent banner under GDPR in most interpretations, which is one of its biggest practical draws for businesses trying to simplify privacy compliance while still getting reliable traffic analytics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Plausible's hosted cloud product is priced by monthly pageviews rather than seats: Starter begins at USD 9 per month for up to 10,000 monthly pageviews, Growth begins at USD 14 per month with support for more sites and team members, and Business begins at USD 19 per month with custom properties, a Stats API, funnels and revenue attribution.

Because the software is open source under the AGPLv3 license, organizations that prefer not to pay a subscription can self-host Plausible on their own infrastructure at no license cost, trading the convenience of the managed cloud service for the effort of running and maintaining it themselves.

features.title: Cookieless tracking
features.description: Collects traffic data without cookies, so most sites do not need a cookie consent banner for analytics.

features.title: Single-page dashboard
features.description: Presents key metrics like visitors, referrers, top pages and bounce rate on one simple, fast-loading dashboard.

features.title: Open-source and self-hostable
features.description: Fully open source under AGPLv3, with the option to self-host instead of using the managed cloud service.

features.title: GDPR and CCPA compliant by design
features.description: Collects no personally identifiable information and processes data entirely within the EU.

features.title: Pageview-based pricing
features.description: Cloud plans are priced by monthly pageview volume rather than per-seat licensing.

features.title: Goals, funnels and revenue attribution
features.description: Tracks conversion goals, multi-step funnels and revenue attribution on higher-tier plans.

features.title: Stats API and integrations
features.description: Provides a Stats API and a Looker Studio connector for pulling analytics data into other tools.

pros: Strong privacy focus with no cookies or personal data collection
Lightweight, fast-loading tracking script compared to larger analytics suites
Simple, single-page dashboard that is easy for non-analysts to read
Open source with a free self-hosting option for full data control
Transparent, pageview-based pricing rather than opaque enterprise quotes
cons: Fewer advanced segmentation and behavioral analysis features than Google Analytics
Self-hosting requires ongoing server maintenance and updates
Higher-tier features like funnels and revenue attribution require the pricier Business plan
Smaller ecosystem of third-party integrations than more established analytics platforms

faqs.question: Is Plausible Analytics really cookieless?
faqs.answer: Yes, Plausible does not use cookies or collect personally identifiable information to track website visitors.

faqs.question: Is Plausible open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Plausible Analytics is open source under the GNU Affero General Public License v3 (AGPLv3), and it can be self-hosted for free.

faqs.question: How much does Plausible cost?
faqs.answer: Cloud pricing starts at USD 9 per month for the Starter plan covering up to 10,000 monthly pageviews, with Growth and Business plans starting at USD 14 and USD 19 per month respectively.

faqs.question: Who founded Plausible Analytics?
faqs.answer: Plausible was founded by Uku Taht in December 2018, with Marko Saric joining in 2020 to lead marketing and communications.

faqs.question: Where is Plausible based?
faqs.answer: Plausible Insights OU is headquartered in Tartu, Estonia, and all data is hosted and processed within the EU.

faqs.question: Does Plausible require a cookie consent banner?
faqs.answer: Because Plausible does not use cookies or collect personal data, most sites using it do not need to show a cookie consent banner under common GDPR interpretations.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Plausible?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Google Analytics, Fathom Analytics, Simple Analytics and Matomo.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Plausible for free?
faqs.answer: Yes, because it is open source under AGPLv3, Plausible can be self-hosted at no license cost, though you are responsible for server maintenance.

use_cases.title: GDPR-friendly website analytics
use_cases.description: Businesses in the EU and privacy-regulated industries use Plausible to track traffic without cookie banners or personal data collection.

use_cases.title: Lightweight traffic tracking for developers
use_cases.description: Developers and indie makers add a small, fast script to track visitors without slowing down page load times.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted analytics for data control
use_cases.description: Organizations self-host Plausible on their own infrastructure to keep full ownership of visitor data.

features: Stats API, funnels and revenue attribution
